title: Minimum cost path in weighted grid.
description: Minimum cost path in weighted grid.

examples.explanation: Start at (0,0) with cost=1. From (0,0): Option 1: Move right to (0,1) cost=1+3=4. From (0,1): Option 1a: Move right to (0,2) cost=4+1=5. From (0,2): Move down to (1,2) cost=5+1=6. From (1,2): Move down to (2,2) cost=6+1=7 (destination). Option 1b: From (0,1): Move down to (1,1) cost=4+5=9. From (1,1): Option 1b-i: Move right to (1,2) cost=9+1=10. From (1,2): Move down to (2,2) cost=10+1=11. Option 1b-ii: From (1,1): Move down to (2,1) cost=9+2=11. From (2,1): Move right to (2,2) cost=11+1=12. Option 2: From (0,0): Move down to (1,0) cost=1+1=2. From (1,0): Option 2a: Move right to (1,1) cost=2+5=7. From (1,1): Option 2a-i: Move right to (1,2) cost=7+1=8. From (1,2): Move down to (2,2) cost=8+1=9. Option 2a-ii: From (1,1): Move down to (2,1) cost=7+2=9. From (2,1): Move right to (2,2) cost=9+1=10. Option 2b: From (1,0): Move down to (2,0) cost=2+4=6. From (2,0): Move right to (2,1) cost=6+2=8. From (2,1): Move right to (2,2) cost=8+1=9. Minimum among all paths is 7 (path: right→right→down→down).

Start at (0,0) with cost=1. From (0,0): Option 1: Move right to (0,1) cost=1+2=3. From (0,1): Move down to (1,1) cost=3+4=7 (destination). Option 2: Move down to (1,0) cost=1+3=4. From (1,0): Move right to (1,1) cost=4+4=8. Minimum is 7.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursive O(2^(m+n))
approaches.summary: Explore all possible paths from top-left to bottom-right by recursively moving right or down at each cell, summing costs, and tracking the minimum. This is straightforward but highly inefficient for larger grids due to exponential time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursive Minimum Cost Path
ladders.desc: Complete implementation exploring all possible paths from top-left to bottom-right in a weighted grid.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force recursive solution to find the minimum cost path from the top-left corner (0,0) to the bottom-right corner (m-1,n-1) in an m×n grid where each cell has a non-negative cost. The intuition is simple: at each cell, we can only move right or down (typical grid path constraints), so we recursively explore both possibilities, accumulating costs along the way. When we reach the destination, we return the accumulated cost. By exploring all possible paths (2^(m+n-2) possibilities since we need m-1 down moves and n-1 right moves), we can find the minimum cost. The algorithm starts at (0,0) with initial cost grid[0][0]. At each position (i,j), if we're at the destination (m-1,n-1), return the current accumulated cost. Otherwise, recursively explore moving right (if within bounds) and down (if within bounds), taking the minimum of the two results. The time complexity is O(2^(m+n)) because each recursive call branches into two possibilities (right/down), creating an exponential recursion tree. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the recursion call stack depth (maximum path length). This approach is straightforward but highly inefficient for grids larger than about 15×15 due to exponential explosion. Common mistakes include: forgetting base cases (boundary checks), incorrectly handling the starting cell's cost, or using inefficient data structures that add overhead. The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the recursive helper function as its core component.

examples.trace: Start at (0,0) with cost=1. From (0,0): Option 1: Move right to (0,1) cost=1+3=4. From (0,1): Option 1a: Move right to (0,2) cost=4+1=5. From (0,2): Move down to (1,2) cost=5+1=6. From (1,2): Move down to (2,2) cost=6+1=7 (destination). Option 1b: From (0,1): Move down to (1,1) cost=4+5=9. From (1,1): Option 1b-i: Move right to (1,2) cost=9+1=10. From (1,2): Move down to (2,2) cost=10+1=11. Option 1b-ii: From (1,1): Move down to (2,1) cost=9+2=11. From (2,1): Move right to (2,2) cost=11+1=12. Option 2: From (0,0): Move down to (1,0) cost=1+1=2. From (1,0): Option 2a: Move right to (1,1) cost=2+5=7. From (1,1): Option 2a-i: Move right to (1,2) cost=7+1=8. From (1,2): Move down to (2,2) cost=8+1=9. Option 2a-ii: From (1,1): Move down to (2,1) cost=7+2=9. From (2,1): Move right to (2,2) cost=9+1=10. Option 2b: From (1,0): Move down to (2,0) cost=2+4=6. From (2,0): Move right to (2,1) cost=6+2=8. From (2,1): Move right to (2,2) cost=8+1=9. Minimum among all paths is 7 (path: right→right→down→down).

Start at (0,0) with cost=1. From (0,0): Option 1: Move right to (0,1) cost=1+2=3. From (0,1): Move down to (1,1) cost=3+4=7 (destination). Option 2: Move down to (1,0) cost=1+3=4. From (1,0): Move right to (1,1) cost=4+4=8. Minimum is 7.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Path Explorer Function
ladders.desc: The recursive helper function that explores all paths from a given cell to the destination.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive function that solves the subproblem: given a current position (i,j) in an m×n grid and the accumulated cost so far, find the minimum cost to reach the destination (m-1,n-1) from this point. The intuition is divide-and-conquer: the minimum cost from (i,j) equals the cost at (i,j) plus the minimum of the minimum costs from the cell to the right (if exists) and the cell below (if exists). This recursive relationship breaks the problem into smaller subproblems. The algorithm: function minCostFrom(i,j,currentCost): 1. If i==m-1 and j==n-1 (destination), return currentCost + grid[i][j]. 2. Initialize minCost = infinity. 3. If j+1 < n (can move right), recursively call minCostFrom(i,j+1,currentCost+grid[i][j]) and update minCost with the result. 4. If i+1 < m (can move down), recursively call minCostFrom(i+1,j,currentCost+grid[i][j]) and update minCost with the result. 5. Return minCost. Time complexity for this subproblem from a given (i,j) is O(2^( (m-i)+(n-j) )) because it explores all paths from that point. Space complexity is O((m-i)+(n-j)) for recursion depth. Common mistakes: not adding current cell's cost before recursion, incorrect base case (should include current cell's cost), or forgetting to handle cases where only one direction is possible. This feeds into L0 as the main recursive engine called from the top-level function starting at (0,0) with initial cost 0.

examples.trace: At (0,0) with currentCost=0. Cell cost=1. Option 1: Move right to (0,1). Call minCostFrom(0,1,0+1=1). At (0,1): cell cost=2, currentCost=1. At destination? No (0,1≠1,1). Option 1a: Move down to (1,1). Call minCostFrom(1,1,1+2=3). At (1,1): cell cost=4, currentCost=3. At destination? Yes (1,1). Return 3+4=7. Option 2: From (0,0): Move down to (1,0). Call minCostFrom(1,0,0+1=1). At (1,0): cell cost=3, currentCost=1. Option 2a: Move right to (1,1). Call minCostFrom(1,1,1+3=4). At (1,1): cell cost=4, currentCost=4. At destination? Yes. Return 4+4=8. Return min(7,8)=7.
At (0,0) with currentCost=0. Cell cost=5. At destination? Yes (0,0 is only cell). Return 0+5=5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Decomposition of Path Problem
ladders.desc: The fundamental recursive relationship: minCost(i,j) = cost(i,j) + min(minCost(i,j+1), minCost(i+1,j)).
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core recursive mathematical relationship that drives the brute force approach. The intuition is that to find the minimum cost path from any cell (i,j) to the destination, we don't need to think about the entire path at once—we can break it down into immediate next steps. The minimum cost from (i,j) is simply the cost of standing on that cell plus the minimum of the minimum costs from the cell to the right (if we move right) and the cell below (if we move down). This works because moving right or down are the only allowed moves, and the problem has optimal substructure: an optimal path from (i,j) must consist of an optimal move to either (i,j+1) or (i+1,j), followed by an optimal path from that next cell. The algorithm is expressed as a recurrence relation: minCost(i,j) = grid[i][j] + min( minCost(i,j+1) if j+1<n else ∞, minCost(i+1,j) if i+1<m else ∞ ), with base case minCost(m-1,n-1) = grid[m-1][n-1]. This is a recursive definition without implementation details. Time analysis: evaluating this recurrence naively leads to exponential time because each minCost(i,j) depends on two subproblems, causing overlapping subproblems (though not exploited here). Space: recursion depth up to m+n. Common mistakes: misunderstanding the base case (should be at destination, not when out of bounds), incorrect handling of boundary cells (should return a large value like infinity when no move possible), or not adding current cell's cost. This feeds into L1 as the conceptual foundation for the recursive helper function.

examples.trace: Compute minCost(0,0): grid[0][0]=1. minCost(0,1)=grid[0][1]+minCost(1,1) because from (0,1) only down move possible. minCost(1,1)=grid[1][1]=4. So minCost(0,1)=2+4=6. minCost(1,0)=grid[1][0]+minCost(1,1)=3+4=7. Thus minCost(0,0)=1+min(6,7)=1+6=7.
Base case: at destination (0,0), so minCost(0,0)=grid[0][0]=5.

testCases.expected: minCost(1,1)=5+min(minCost(1,2), minCost(2,1)) but (2,1) out of bounds, so minCost(1,1)=5+minCost(1,2)=5+6=11

ladders.title: Building Block — Grid Traversal with Constrained Moves
ladders.desc: Navigating a 2D grid allowing only right and down movements from any cell.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves traversing a 2D grid of size m×n where from any cell (i,j), you can only move to (i,j+1) (right) or (i+1,j) (down), provided those cells exist within grid bounds. This is a fundamental pattern in grid path problems. The intuition is that such constraints create a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of moves, preventing cycles (since you can't move left or up), which simplifies path counting and cost accumulation. The algorithm for enumerating all paths from (0,0) to (m-1,n-1) using recursion: function traverse(i,j,path): 1. If i==m-1 and j==n-1, record path as complete. 2. If j+1 < n, recursively call traverse(i,j+1,path + 'right'). 3. If i+1 < m, recursively call traverse(i+1,j,path + 'down'). This generates all possible sequences of m-1 down moves and n-1 right moves (total moves = m+n-2). The number of paths is combinatorial: C((m-1)+(n-1), m-1) = C(m+n-2, m-1), which is exponential in m,n. Time complexity for enumeration is O(2^(m+n)) in worst case. Space: O(m+n) for recursion depth. Common mistakes: moving out of bounds without checks, incorrect base case condition, or using inefficient path storage. This feeds into L2 by providing the movement framework upon which cost accumulation is added.

examples.trace: Start at (0,0). Option 1: Move right to (0,1). From (0,1): only down move to (1,1) (destination). Path: right→down. Option 2: Move down to (1,0). From (1,0): only right move to (1,1). Path: down→right. Total 2 paths.
Start at (0,0). Only right moves possible: to (0,1), then to (0,2). Path: right→right. Total 1 path.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing and Summing Grid Cell Values
ladders.desc: Retrieving values from a 2D grid and accumulating them in a sequence.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves two key skills: 1) accessing an element in a 2D list/array given row and column indices, and 2) summing a sequence of numbers. In the context of our problem, we need to access the cost value at each grid cell (i,j) using grid[i][j] (0-indexed), and accumulate these values along a path. The intuition is simple: the total cost of a path is the sum of the costs of all cells visited along that path. For a path from (0,0) to (m-1,n-1) with only right/down moves, exactly m+n-1 cells are visited (including start and end). The algorithm: initialize totalCost = 0. For each cell (i,j) in the path in order, totalCost += grid[i][j]. This is a linear-time operation per path. Time complexity: O(m+n) for one path. Space: O(1) for the accumulator. Common mistakes: off-by-one errors in indexing (e.g., using 1-indexing when grid is 0-indexed), forgetting to include the starting cell's cost, or incorrect accumulation order. This feeds into L3 by providing the cost calculation mechanism that turns grid traversal into a cost accumulation problem.

examples.trace: totalCost=0. Visit (0,0): grid[0][0]=1, totalCost=0+1=1. Visit (0,1): grid[0][1]=2, totalCost=1+2=3. Visit (1,1): grid[1][1]=4, totalCost=3+4=7.
totalCost=0. Visit (0,0): grid[0][0]=5, totalCost=0+5=5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding 2D Grid Coordinates and Indices
ladders.desc: Basic concept of representing positions in a grid using row and column indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: understanding how a 2D grid (matrix) is represented in programming, typically as a list of lists or a 2D array. Each cell in the grid has a position specified by two indices: row index (i) and column index (j). In 0-indexed systems (most common), the top-left corner is (0,0), rows increase downward, and columns increase rightward. For an m×n grid (m rows, n columns), valid indices are i from 0 to m-1 and j from 0 to n-1. The intuition is that we can uniquely identify any cell with these two numbers, similar to coordinates on a map. This foundation is crucial for navigating the grid: moving right increases j by 1, moving down increases i by 1. The algorithm for accessing a value at (i,j) is simply grid[i][j]. Time and space: O(1) for access. Common mistakes: confusing rows and columns (thinking i is column, j is row), using 1-indexing when the grid is 0-indexed, or accessing out-of-bounds indices. This feeds into L4 by providing the coordinate system needed to access cell values.

examples.trace: Grid has 2 rows (i=0,1) and 2 columns (j=0,1). Position (0,1) means row 0, column 1. Row 0 is [1,2]. Column 1 in this row is the second element: 2.
Grid has 2 rows: row0=[5,6,7], row1=[8,9,10]. Position (1,2) means row 1, column 2. In row1, column 2 is the third element: 10.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Use a 2D DP table where each cell stores the minimum cost to reach that cell from the top-left, computed by taking the minimum of the costs from the top and left neighbors plus the current cell's cost. This avoids redundant calculations by building solutions iteratively.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Cost Path in Weighted Grid using DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find minimum cost path from top-left to bottom-right in a weighted grid using 2D DP.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires finding the minimum cost to traverse from the top-left corner (0,0) to the bottom-right corner (m-1,n-1) of an m×n grid, where each cell has a non-negative cost, and you can only move right or down. The dynamic programming approach builds a 2D DP table where dp[i][j] stores the minimum cost to reach cell (i,j) from (0,0). The intuition is that to reach any cell, you must come from either the cell above (i-1,j) or the cell to the left (i,j-1), so the minimum cost is the minimum of these two predecessor costs plus the current cell's cost. This avoids exponential recomputation by solving subproblems iteratively. The algorithm initializes dp[0][0] = grid[0][0], then fills the first row and first column (since they only have one possible incoming direction), and finally fills the rest of the table using the recurrence: dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). The answer is dp[m-1][n-1]. Time complexity is O(m*n) as we compute each cell once, and space complexity is O(m*n) for the DP table. This connects to L1 by using the core recurrence as the key subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases for the first row/column correctly, using incorrect indices, or misunderstanding the movement constraints (only right/down). Real code pattern involves nested loops to fill the DP table.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Recurrence for Minimum Cost Path
ladders.desc: Implement the recurrence relation to compute minimum cost for each cell based on top and left neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the core recurrence relation: dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]), which calculates the minimum cost to reach cell (i,j) from the top-left corner. The intuition is that since movement is restricted to right or down, any path to (i,j) must pass through either the cell directly above (i-1,j) or directly to the left (i,j-1). Therefore, the minimum cost to reach (i,j) is the minimum of the costs to reach these two predecessor cells, plus the cost of the current cell itself. This recurrence avoids recomputing paths by building solutions from smaller subproblems. The algorithm assumes dp is a 2D array initialized with base cases (first row and column handled separately in the full problem). For each cell (i,j) where i>0 and j>0, apply the recurrence. Time complexity for this sub-routine over all cells is O(m*n), but for a single cell it's O(1). Space complexity is O(1) extra per cell. This feeds into L0 as the central logic used to fill the DP table after handling boundaries. Common mistakes include accessing indices out of bounds (e.g., when i=0 or j=0), or incorrectly using max instead of min. Real code pattern is a simple assignment with a min function call.

examples.input: grid = [[1,3,1],[1,5,1],[4,2,1]], dp pre-filled for first row/col: dp[0]=[1,4,5], dp[1][0]=2, dp[2][0]=6, compute dp[1][1]
examples.trace: For cell (1,1): grid[1][1] = 5. Top neighbor dp[0][1] = 4. Left neighbor dp[1][0] = 2. min(4,2) = 2. dp[1][1] = 5 + 2 = 7.
For cell (1,1): grid[1][1] = 5. Top neighbor dp[0][1] = 3. Left neighbor dp[1][0] = 5. min(3,5) = 3. dp[1][1] = 5 + 3 = 8.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Minimum of Two Values in Path Cost Context
ladders.desc: Determine the minimum cost between two possible incoming paths (top vs left) for a grid cell.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the decision-making aspect: given the costs to reach the top neighbor and left neighbor of a cell, choose the smaller one to minimize the total path cost. The intuition is that in a grid where movement is only right or down, the optimal path to any cell must come from the cheaper of the two adjacent cells that can reach it. This is a greedy-like choice within the DP framework, ensuring local optimality leads to global optimum. The algorithm is straightforward: for a cell at (i,j), if dp_top is the minimum cost to reach (i-1,j) and dp_left is for (i,j-1), then the incoming cost is min(dp_top, dp_left). This operation is performed for each cell (except boundaries) in the DP table. Time complexity is O(1) per cell, and space is O(1) extra. This connects to L1 as it's embedded in the recurrence relation. Common mistakes include comparing the wrong values (e.g., grid costs instead of dp values) or not considering that both neighbors must exist (handled in higher levels). Real code pattern uses the min function on two integers.

examples.trace: Compute min(4, 2) = 2. This means the path coming from the left is cheaper than from the top.
Compute min(3, 5) = 3. This means the path from the top is cheaper.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterative 2D Array Traversal
ladders.desc: Traverse a 2D grid in row-major order to fill values based on previous computations.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves systematically iterating through a 2D array (grid or DP table) to compute values where each value depends on previously computed neighbors. The intuition is that to apply dynamic programming efficiently, we must process cells in an order that ensures when we compute dp[i][j], the values dp[i-1][j] (top) and dp[i][j-1] (left) are already known. Row-major order (nested loops: outer for rows i from 0 to m-1, inner for columns j from 0 to n-1) naturally satisfies this because as we move left to right and top to bottom, the top and left neighbors are visited earlier. The algorithm initializes the DP table, then uses two nested loops to fill it. For boundary cells (first row or first column), special handling is needed since they lack one neighbor. Time complexity is O(m*n) for the traversal, and space is O(m*n) for the table. This connects to L2 by providing the framework where the min operation is applied at each cell. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds, traversing in reverse order, or not initializing base cases. Real code pattern uses for-loops with range.

examples.trace: Initialize dp[1][3]. Set dp[0][0]=1. First row only: i=0, j=1: dp[0][1]=2+1=3. j=2: dp[0][2]=3+3=6.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing Grid Cells and Neighbors
ladders.desc: Retrieve values from a 2D grid using indices and access adjacent cells (top and left).
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves working with a 2D array (list of lists in Python) to get the value at a specific cell (i,j) and its immediate neighbors: the top neighbor at (i-1,j) and left neighbor at (i,j-1). The intuition is that in grid-based problems, data is stored in a structured way where rows and columns are indexed starting from 0, and we often need to look at surrounding cells to make decisions. The algorithm uses simple array indexing: grid[i][j] gives the cost at cell (i,j). For neighbors, we access grid[i-1][j] and grid[i][j-1], but must ensure indices are within bounds (i>0 for top, j>0 for left). This operation is basic but critical for building the DP solution. Time complexity is O(1) per access, and space is O(1) extra. This connects to L3 as it's used during traversal to read grid costs and DP values. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors, confusing rows and columns, or not checking bounds before access. Real code pattern uses square brackets for indexing.

examples.input: grid = [[1,2],[3,4]], access cell (0,1) and its neighbors
examples.trace: grid[0][1] = 2. Top neighbor: i-1 = -1, so out of bounds (no top). Left neighbor: grid[0][0] = 1.

examples.input: grid = [[5,6,7],[8,9,10]], access cell (1,2) and its neighbors
examples.trace: grid[1][2] = 10. Top neighbor: grid[0][2] = 7. Left neighbor: grid[1][1] = 9.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — 2D Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand and use 2D arrays (matrices) to store grid data with row and column indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a 2D array is a data structure that organizes elements in rows and columns, like a grid. In programming, it's often implemented as a list of lists, where each inner list represents a row. The intuition is that it allows us to model problems with spatial relationships, such as a weighted grid where each cell has a cost. Indexing starts at 0, so the top-left cell is at position (0,0), the cell to its right is (0,1), and below it is (1,0). The algorithm involves creating a 2D array (e.g., in Python: grid = [[1,2],[3,4]]), accessing elements via grid[row][column], and understanding that rows and columns are zero-based. This foundation is essential because the DP approach relies on storing and retrieving costs from such a structure. Time complexity for access is O(1), and space is proportional to the number of cells. This connects to L4 as it's the basis for accessing cells and neighbors. Common mistakes include confusing rows with columns, using 1-based indexing, or not initializing the array correctly. Real code pattern involves nested lists and indexing.

examples.trace: Define grid as a list containing two lists: first row [5,10], second row [15,20]. Access grid[0][0] = 5, grid[0][1] = 10, grid[1][0] = 15, grid[1][1] = 20.

examples.input: Access cell at row 1, column 0 in grid [[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]
examples.trace: Grid has rows: row 0 = [1,2,3], row 1 = [4,5,6]. Column indices: 0,1,2. So grid[1][0] refers to row 1, column 0, which is 4.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming Space-Optimized O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Optimize space by using only two rows or a single row of the DP table, since each cell's value depends only on its top and left neighbors. This reduces memory usage while maintaining the same time complexity as standard DP.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Cost Path with Space-Optimized DP
ladders.desc: Find minimum cost path from top-left to bottom-right in weighted grid using only two rows for DP.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum cost path from the top-left corner (0,0) to the bottom-right corner (m-1,n-1) in an m×n grid where each cell has a non-negative cost. You can only move right or down. The space-optimized dynamic programming approach reduces memory usage from O(m×n) to O(min(m,n)) by recognizing that each cell's value depends only on its top and left neighbors. We maintain only two rows (or one row with careful updating) to compute the DP values. The intuition is that we don't need the entire grid's history—just the previous row and current row suffice because when filling row i, we only need values from row i-1 (top neighbor) and already computed values in row i (left neighbor). This is essentially a rolling array technique. The algorithm: 1) Handle edge cases (empty grid, single row/column). 2) Initialize a DP array of size n (columns) with large values, set dp[0]=grid[0][0]. 3) For each row i from 0 to m-1: a) For column 0, update dp[0] by adding grid[i][0] to previous dp[0] (except first cell). b) For columns j from 1 to n-1: dp[j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[j] (top), dp[j-1] (left)). 4) Return dp[n-1]. Time complexity is O(m×n) as we visit each cell once. Space complexity is O(n) (or O(m) if we transpose for min(m,n)). Connection to L1: This uses the row-by-row computation subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling the first row/column correctly, forgetting to update dp[0] separately, using wrong min comparison, or not resetting for new rows properly. Real code pattern: def minPathSum(grid): m,n=len(grid),len(grid[0]); dp=[float('inf')]*n; dp[0]=grid[0][0]; for i in range(m): for j in range(n): if i==0 and j==0: continue; if j==0: dp[j]+=grid[i][j]; else: dp[j]=grid[i][j]+min(dp[j], dp[j-1]); return dp[-1].

examples.trace: Initialize dp=[inf,inf,inf], set dp[0]=1. Row 0: j=0 skip, j=1: dp[1]=2+min(inf,1)=3, j=2: dp[2]=3+min(inf,3)=6. dp=[1,3,6]. Row 1: j=0: dp[0]=1+4=5, j=1: dp[1]=5+min(3,5)=8, j=2: dp[2]=6+min(6,8)=12. Return dp[2]=12.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Row-by-Row DP Update with Two Rows
ladders.desc: Compute minimum path cost for current row using previous row's DP values.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the space-optimized approach. Given a grid row and the previous row's DP values (representing minimum costs to reach each column in the previous row), compute the current row's DP values. The intuition: For each cell (i,j), the minimum cost to reach it is grid[i][j] plus the minimum of the cost from above (i-1,j) and left (i,j-1). Since we process rows sequentially, when computing row i, we have previous row's dp_prev[j] values (top neighbors) and as we move left to right in the current row, we compute dp_curr[j] using dp_curr[j-1] (left neighbor, already computed) and dp_prev[j] (top neighbor). This allows using only two arrays. Algorithm: 1) Initialize dp_curr as copy of dp_prev (or large values). 2) For column 0: dp_curr[0] = dp_prev[0] + grid[i][0] (since no left neighbor). 3) For columns j from 1 to n-1: dp_curr[j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp_prev[j], dp_curr[j-1]). 4) Return dp_curr as new dp_prev for next row. Time: O(n) per row, total O(m×n). Space: O(n) for two rows. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each row in the full solution. Common mistakes: Not updating column 0 correctly, mixing up dp_prev and dp_curr indices, or not handling first row specially. Real code pattern: def update_row(grid_row, dp_prev): n=len(grid_row); dp_curr=[0]*n; dp_curr[0]=dp_prev[0]+grid_row[0]; for j in range(1,n): dp_curr[j]=grid_row[j]+min(dp_prev[j], dp_curr[j-1]); return dp_curr.

examples.trace: Initialize dp_curr=[0,0,0]. Column 0: dp_curr[0]=dp_prev[0]+grid_row[0]=1+1=2. Column 1: dp_curr[1]=grid_row[1]+min(dp_prev[1]=4, dp_curr[0]=2)=5+2=7. Column 2: dp_curr[2]=grid_row[2]+min(dp_prev[2]=5, dp_curr[1]=7)=1+5=6. Return [2,7,6].
dp_curr=[0,0,0]. Column 0: dp_curr[0]=1+4=5. Column 1: dp_curr[1]=5+min(3,5)=8. Column 2: dp_curr[2]=6+min(6,8)=12. Return [5,8,12].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Dependency on Top and Left Neighbors Only
ladders.desc: Understand why each cell's value depends only on top and left cells in DP.
ladders.explanation: This core insight enables space optimization. In the minimum cost path problem with moves only right or down, the cost to reach cell (i,j) is determined solely by its top neighbor (i-1,j) and left neighbor (i,j-1). Why? Because any path to (i,j) must come from either above or left—you cannot move up or left, so those are the only possible immediate predecessors. Formally, let dp[i][j] = minimum cost to reach (i,j). Then dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]) for i>0, j>0. For edges: dp[0][j] = grid[0][j] + dp[0][j-1] (only left), dp[i][0] = grid[i][0] + dp[i-1][0] (only top). This recurrence shows that to compute dp[i][j], we only need values from the same row (left) and previous row (top). Thus, we don't need to store the entire 2D table—just the current row and previous row suffice. This reduces space from O(m×n) to O(n). The intuition is similar to rolling arrays in DP where we overwrite old values. Time analysis: Each cell computed once, O(1) operations, so O(m×n). Space: O(n) for two rows. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the row update subroutine. Common mistakes: Thinking dependencies include diagonal or other cells, or not handling boundary conditions. Real code pattern: The recurrence itself: if i==0 and j==0: dp=grid[0][0]; elif i==0: dp[j]=dp[j-1]+grid[i][j]; elif j==0: dp[j]=dp[j]+grid[i][j]; else: dp[j]=grid[i][j]+min(dp[j], dp[j-1]).

examples.trace: grid[1][1]=5. Top neighbor dp[0][1]=4, left neighbor dp[1][0]=2. min(4,2)=2. dp[1][1]=5+2=7.
grid[0][2]=3. No top neighbor (i=0), left neighbor dp[0][1]=3. dp[0][2]=3+3=6.

ladders.title: Building Block — 1D DP Array for Row Processing
ladders.desc: Maintain a 1D array to store minimum costs for each column across rows.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves using a single 1D array (e.g., dp of size n) to store the current minimum costs as we process the grid row by row. Initially, dp[j] represents the minimum cost to reach column j in the current row being processed. After processing a row, dp is updated to reflect values for that row, and then reused for the next row. The key insight is that we can overwrite dp in-place because when computing a new row, we need old dp values (from previous row) for top neighbors, but as we compute left to right, we update dp[j] using dp[j-1] (which is already updated for current row) and the old dp[j] (from previous row, stored before overwriting). To avoid losing the old value, we can either use two arrays or carefully update: for column 0, dp[0] += grid[i][0]; for j>0, dp[j] = grid[i][j] + min(old_dp[j], dp[j-1]), where old_dp[j] is the value before update. In practice, we often use two arrays (prev and curr) for clarity. This building block teaches how to compress 2D DP into 1D. Time: O(n) per row. Space: O(n). Connection to L2: This array implements the dependency logic. Common mistakes: Not initializing dp properly, incorrectly updating in-place without saving old values, or mishandling the first column. Real code pattern: dp=[float('inf')]*n; dp[0]=grid[0][0]; for i in range(m): if i>0: dp[0]+=grid[i][0]; for j in range(1,n): dp[j]=grid[i][j]+min(dp[j], dp[j-1]).

examples.trace: Start dp=[1,4,5]. For column 0: dp[0]=dp[0]+1=1+1=2. Column 1: dp[1]=5+min(old dp[1]=4, dp[0]=2)=5+2=7. Column 2: dp[2]=1+min(old dp[2]=5, dp[1]=7)=1+5=6. Final dp=[2,7,6].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Min of Two Numbers and Addition
ladders.desc: Compute grid cell value plus minimum of two adjacent DP values.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation is the atomic step in the DP recurrence: for a cell with cost C, and two neighbor costs A (top) and B (left), compute C + min(A, B). This operation combines the cell's own cost with the cheapest way to reach it from the allowed directions. The intuition is simple: to minimize total cost, at each step choose the cheaper incoming path. This is applied repeatedly across the grid. In pseudocode: result = grid[i][j] + min(top, left). Time: O(1) per cell. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is performed for each cell when updating the 1D DP array. Common mistakes: Using max instead of min, adding before taking min, or mishandling when one neighbor is absent (on edges). Real code pattern: def cell_cost(c, a, b): return c + min(a, b) if a is not None and b is not None else (c + a if b is None else c + b) for edge cases.

examples.trace: B is None (no left neighbor), so use A only: 3+1=4.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Minimum of Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Find the smaller of two numbers, a prerequisite for path cost comparison.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: given two numbers, determine which is smaller (or if equal, either). In the context of the minimum cost path, we use this to choose between the cost from the top neighbor and the cost from the left neighbor—pick the cheaper one to minimize total cost. The intuition is straightforward: if you have two options, selecting the minimum leads to an overall minimum when combined with additional costs. For example, if top cost is 3 and left cost is 5, choosing top (3) is better. Algorithm: compare a and b using a comparison operator; if a <= b, min is a, else min is b. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L4: This min operation is part of the basic cell cost computation. Common mistakes: Confusing min with max, or not handling negative numbers (but costs are non-negative here). Real code pattern: def min_two(a, b): return a if a <= b else b.

examples.trace: Compare 3 and 5: 3 <= 5 is true, so min is 3.
Compare 7 and 2: 7 <= 2 is false, so min is 2.

approaches.name: Dijkstra's Algorithm O((m*n) log(m*n))
approaches.summary: Treat the grid as a graph where each cell is a node with edges to its right and down neighbors, weighted by the destination cell's cost. Use Dijkstra's algorithm with a priority queue to find the shortest path from top-left to bottom-right, handling non-negative weights efficiently.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Cost Path in Weighted Grid using Dijkstra's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of Dijkstra's algorithm to find minimum cost path from top-left to bottom-right in a weighted grid.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the minimum cost path from the top-left cell (0,0) to the bottom-right cell (m-1,n-1) in a grid where each cell has a non-negative cost. We can model this as a graph problem: each cell is a node, and edges exist to adjacent cells (right and down) with weights equal to the destination cell's cost. Dijkstra's algorithm is ideal because it efficiently finds shortest paths in graphs with non-negative weights. The intuition is to maintain a priority queue of cells to explore, always processing the cell with the smallest known distance first, which guarantees that once we reach the bottom-right cell, we've found the minimum cost path. The algorithm starts by setting the distance to the start cell as its own cost (since you must pay to enter it), and all other distances as infinity. We use a min-heap priority queue to always expand the cell with the smallest current distance. For each cell popped from the heap, we examine its right and down neighbors (if within bounds), calculate the new distance as current distance plus neighbor's cost, and if this is smaller than the known distance, update it and push to the heap. This continues until we pop the bottom-right cell from the heap, at which point its distance is the minimum cost. Time complexity is O((m*n) log(m*n)) because each cell can be pushed/popped from the heap, and heap operations are logarithmic. Space complexity is O(m*n) for the distance array and heap. Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of Dijkstra's relaxation step to update distances. Common mistakes include forgetting to include the start cell's cost, incorrectly handling boundaries, or using a regular queue instead of a priority queue. Real code pattern: Initialize distances with infinity, set dist[0][0] = grid[0][0], use heap with (dist, row, col), while heap not empty pop smallest, if popped is destination return dist, for each neighbor calculate newDist = currDist + grid[nr][nc], if newDist < dist[nr][nc] update and push to heap.

examples.trace: Start: dist[0][0]=1, heap=[(1,0,0)]. Pop (1,0,0): neighbors (0,1) newDist=1+3=4 < inf, update dist[0][1]=4, push (4,0,1); (1,0) newDist=1+1=2 < inf, update dist[1][0]=2, push (2,1,0). Heap now [(2,1,0),(4,0,1)]. Pop (2,1,0): neighbors (1,1) newDist=2+5=7 < inf, update dist[1][1]=7, push (7,1,1); (2,0) newDist=2+4=6 < inf, update dist[2][0]=6, push (6,2,0). Heap now [(4,0,1),(6,2,0),(7,1,1)]. Pop (4,0,1): neighbors (0,2) newDist=4+1=5 < inf, update dist[0][2]=5, push (5,0,2); (1,1) newDist=4+5=9 >7, skip. Heap now [(5,0,2),(6,2,0),(7,1,1)]. Pop (5,0,2): neighbors (1,2) newDist=5+1=6 < inf, update dist[1][2]=6, push (6,1,2). Heap now [(6,1,2),(6,2,0),(7,1,1)]. Pop (6,1,2): neighbor (2,2) newDist=6+1=7 < inf, update dist[2][2]=7, push (7,2,2). Heap now [(6,2,0),(7,1,1),(7,2,2)]. Pop (6,2,0): neighbor (2,1) newDist=6+2=8 > inf? dist[2][1] still inf, update dist[2][1]=8, push (8,2,1). Heap now [(7,1,1),(7,2,2),(8,2,1)]. Pop (7,1,1): neighbor (2,1) newDist=7+2=9 >8, skip; (1,2) already processed. Heap now [(7,2,2),(8,2,1)]. Pop (7,2,2): this is destination, return 7.

Start: dist[0][0]=1, heap=[(1,0,0)]. Pop (1,0,0): neighbors (0,1) newDist=1+2=3, update dist[0][1]=3, push (3,0,1); (1,0) newDist=1+4=5, update dist[1][0]=5, push (5,1,0). Heap [(3,0,1),(5,1,0)]. Pop (3,0,1): neighbors (0,2) newDist=3+3=6, update dist[0][2]=6, push (6,0,2); (1,1) newDist=3+5=8, update dist[1][1]=8, push (8,1,1). Heap [(5,1,0),(6,0,2),(8,1,1)]. Pop (5,1,0): neighbor (1,1) newDist=5+5=10 >8, skip. Heap [(6,0,2),(8,1,1)]. Pop (6,0,2): neighbor (1,2) newDist=6+6=12, update dist[1][2]=12, push (12,1,2). Heap [(8,1,1),(12,1,2)]. Pop (8,1,1): neighbor (1,2) newDist=8+6=14 >12, skip. Heap [(12,1,2)]. Pop (12,1,2): this is destination, return 12.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Dijkstra's Relaxation Step for Grid Neighbors
ladders.desc: Implement the core relaxation step that updates distances to neighboring cells in the grid.
ladders.explanation: The relaxation step is the heart of Dijkstra's algorithm, where we potentially improve the shortest known distance to a node. In the grid context, for a current cell (r,c) with known distance dist[r][c], we examine its immediate neighbors (right: (r,c+1) and down: (r+1,c)). For each valid neighbor (within grid bounds), we calculate a candidate distance: newDist = dist[r][c] + grid[nr][nc], where grid[nr][nc] is the cost to enter that neighbor cell. If newDist is less than the currently stored distance for that neighbor (initially infinity), we update dist[nr][nc] to newDist and push (newDist, nr, nc) onto the priority queue. This step embodies the greedy choice of Dijkstra's: by always processing the cell with smallest distance first, any update via relaxation guarantees we've found the shortest path to that neighbor so far. The intuition is that if we've found a cheaper way to reach a neighbor via the current cell, we should record it and consider that neighbor for further expansion. Time complexity for this step is O(1) per neighbor, but it's called for each cell processed. Space complexity involves the heap push, which is O(log N). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called repeatedly in the main Dijkstra loop to explore the grid. Common mistakes include updating distances without checking bounds, incorrectly adding costs (e.g., using current cell's cost instead of neighbor's), or not comparing with current distance. Real code pattern: directions = [(0,1),(1,0)]; for dr,dc in directions: nr,nc = r+dr,c+dc; if 0<=nr<m and 0<=nc<n: newDist = dist[r][c] + grid[nr][nc]; if newDist < dist[nr][nc]: dist[nr][nc] = newDist; heapq.heappush(heap, (newDist, nr, nc)).

examples.input: Current cell: (0,0) with dist=5, grid=[[5,2],[3,4]], distances: [[5,inf],[inf,inf]]
examples.trace: For cell (0,0): Check right neighbor (0,1): newDist = 5 + grid[0][1]=5+2=7. Since 7 < inf, update dist[0][1]=7 and push (7,0,1). Check down neighbor (1,0): newDist = 5 + grid[1][0]=5+3=8. Since 8 < inf, update dist[1][0]=8 and push (8,1,0). No other neighbors. Final distances: dist[0][0]=5 (unchanged), dist[0][1]=7, dist[1][0]=8, dist[1][1]=inf.

examples.input: Current cell: (1,0) with dist=8, grid=[[5,2],[3,4]], distances: [[5,7],[8,inf]]
examples.trace: For cell (1,0): Check right neighbor (1,1): newDist = 8 + grid[1][1]=8+4=12. Since 12 < inf, update dist[1][1]=12 and push (12,1,1). Down neighbor (2,0) is out of bounds (grid only 2x2), skip. Final distances: dist[0][0]=5, dist[0][1]=7, dist[1][0]=8, dist[1][1]=12.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Priority Queue Management for Greedy Cell Selection
ladders.desc: Implement and use a min-heap priority queue to always process the cell with the smallest known distance.
ladders.explanation: Dijkstra's algorithm relies on a priority queue (min-heap) to efficiently select the next cell to process. The key insight is that by always expanding the cell with the smallest current distance, we guarantee that once we pop a cell from the heap, its distance is the final shortest path cost. This works because all edge weights are non-negative. We maintain a heap where each element is a tuple (distance, row, col). Initially, we push the start cell (grid[0][0], 0, 0). Then, in a loop, we pop the smallest element from the heap. If this cell is the destination, we can return its distance immediately. Otherwise, we use the relaxation step (L1) to update its neighbors and push any updated neighbors onto the heap. The heap ensures O(log N) time for push/pop operations. The intuition is similar to BFS but with a priority queue instead of a regular queue, adapting to varying costs. Time complexity for heap operations is O(log K) where K is heap size, up to O(log(m*n)). Space complexity is O(m*n) for the heap. Connection to L1: This logic orchestrates the relaxation steps by feeding it the current cell. Common mistakes include using a list and sorting (O(N log N) each time) instead of a heap, not handling duplicate entries in the heap (which is okay as they'll be ignored due to distance checks), or popping without checking if the cell is the destination. Real code pattern: import heapq; heap = []; heapq.heappush(heap, (grid[0][0], 0, 0)); while heap: dist, r, c = heapq.heappop(heap); if (r,c) == (m-1,n-1): return dist; for each neighbor: if newDist < dist[nr][nc]: update and heapq.heappush(heap, (newDist, nr, nc)).

examples.trace: Initial heap: [(1,0,0)]. Pop (1,0,0): not destination, relax neighbors: push (3,0,1) and (4,1,0). Heap now [(3,0,1),(4,1,0)]. Pop (3,0,1): not destination, relax neighbors: from (0,1), push (7,1,1). Heap now [(4,1,0),(7,1,1)]. Pop (4,1,0): not destination, relax neighbors: from (1,0), neighbor (1,1) newDist=4+4=8 >7, skip. Heap now [(7,1,1)]. Pop (7,1,1): this is destination, return 7.

Initial heap: [(0,0,0)]. Pop (0,0,0): not destination, relax neighbors: push (1,0,1) and (1,1,0). Heap now [(1,0,1),(1,1,0)]. Pop (1,0,1): not destination, relax neighbors: from (0,1), neighbor (1,1) newDist=1+0=1, update dist[1][1]=1, push (1,1,1). Heap now [(1,1,0),(1,1,1)]. Pop (1,1,0): not destination, relax neighbors: from (1,0), neighbor (1,1) newDist=1+0=1, but dist[1][1] already 1, skip. Heap now [(1,1,1)]. Pop (1,1,1): this is destination, return 1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation of a Grid with Adjacency
ladders.desc: Model a 2D grid as a graph where cells are nodes and edges connect to right/down neighbors.
ladders.explanation: To apply graph algorithms like Dijkstra's to a grid, we need to represent the grid as a graph. Each cell (r,c) is a node. Edges are directed from a cell to its immediate neighbors: right (r,c+1) and down (r+1,c). The weight of an edge is the cost to enter the destination cell, i.e., grid[nr][nc]. This representation works because moving right or down captures all possible paths from top-left to bottom-right without cycles (since moves only increase row or column indices). The intuition is that the minimum cost path problem reduces to finding the shortest path in this directed acyclic graph (DAG) with non-negative weights. We don't need explicit adjacency lists; we can compute neighbors on-the-fly using indices. This implicit graph has up to 2 edges per node (except boundary cells), totaling O(2*m*n) edges. Time and space for this representation are O(1) per neighbor query. Connection to L2: This graph model defines what nodes and edges the priority queue processes. Common mistakes include considering left/up moves (which could create cycles and aren't needed for this problem), or misassigning edge weights (e.g., using source cell's cost). Real code pattern: For a cell (r,c), neighbors are [(r,c+1), (r+1,c)] if within bounds 0<=nr<m and 0<=nc<n. Edge weight to neighbor (nr,nc) is grid[nr][nc].

examples.trace: Node (0,0): Check right: (0,1) is within bounds (0<=0<2 and 0<=1<2), so neighbor (0,1) with weight grid[0][1]=2. Check down: (1,0) is within bounds, so neighbor (1,0) with weight grid[1][0]=3. No other directions. Total 2 neighbors.
Node (1,2): Check right: (1,3) is out of bounds (nc=3 >= n=3), skip. Check down: (2,2) is out of bounds (nr=2 >= m=2), skip. So no neighbors.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Min-Heap Push and Pop Operations
ladders.desc: Implement fundamental heap operations to maintain a priority queue of (distance, row, col) tuples.
ladders.explanation: A min-heap is a binary tree where the parent node is always smaller than or equal to its children. In Python, we use the heapq module which provides O(log N) push and pop operations. For Dijkstra's, we store tuples (distance, row, col) in the heap. The heap orders primarily by distance (first element), so the smallest distance is always at the root. When pushing, heapq.heappush maintains heap order; when popping, heapq.heappop removes and returns the smallest element. The intuition is that this data structure lets us efficiently retrieve the next cell to process without sorting the entire list each time. Time complexity: push and pop are O(log H) where H is heap size. Space complexity: O(H) for storing elements. Connection to L3: This operation manages the list of cells to explore in the graph representation. Common mistakes include pushing tuples in wrong order (e.g., (row,col,distance) which would sort by row first), or not using heapq and instead manually sorting (which is slower). Real code pattern: import heapq; heap = []; heapq.heappush(heap, (dist, r, c)); dist, r, c = heapq.heappop(heap).

examples.trace: Initial heap: []. Push (5,0,0): heap = [(5,0,0)]. Push (3,0,1): heap adjusts to [(3,0,1),(5,0,0)]. Push (7,1,0): heap = [(3,0,1),(5,0,0),(7,1,0)]. Pop: returns (3,0,1), heap now [(5,0,0),(7,1,0)]. Pop: returns (5,0,0), heap now [(7,1,0)]. Pop: returns (7,1,0), heap empty.
Initial heap: [(1,0,0),(2,1,0),(3,0,1)]. Pop: returns (1,0,0), heap adjusts to [(2,1,0),(3,0,1)]. Pop: returns (2,1,0), heap now [(3,0,1)].

testCases.input: empty heap, pop (should error but assume not called)
push (5,1,2), push (3,2,1), push (4,0,0), pop, push (1,0,1), pop

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Grid Coordinates and Neighbor Indices
ladders.desc: Learn how to navigate a 2D grid using row and column indices and find adjacent cells.
ladders.explanation: A 2D grid is represented as a list of lists, where grid[r][c] gives the value at row r and column c. Rows are indexed from top to bottom (0 to m-1), columns from left to right (0 to n-1). To find neighbors of a cell (r,c), we consider moving in four directions: up (r-1,c), down (r+1,c), left (r,c-1), right (r,c+1). For this problem, we only need right and down moves because we want paths from top-left to bottom-right without going backwards. The intuition is that by only moving right or down, we ensure we make progress toward the destination. To check if a neighbor is within bounds, we verify 0 <= nr < m and 0 <= nc < n. This foundational concept is crucial for mapping the grid to a graph. Time and space are O(1) for these operations. Connection to L4: This coordinate system defines what cells we push onto the heap. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in bounds checking, confusing rows and columns, or using invalid indices. Real code pattern: m = len(grid); n = len(grid[0]); for dr,dc in [(0,1),(1,0)]: nr,nc = r+dr, c+dc; if 0 <= nr < m and 0 <= nc < n: # neighbor is valid.

examples.trace: Grid has m=2 rows, n=2 columns. Cell (0,0): Check right: (0,1) has row 0 (<2) and col 1 (<2), so valid. Check down: (1,0) has row 1 (<2) and col 0 (<2), so valid. Check left: (0,-1) col -1 out of bounds, invalid. Check up: (-1,0) row -1 out of bounds, invalid. So only right and down are valid for this problem.
Grid has m=3, n=3. Cell (2,2): Check right: (2,3) col 3 >= n=3, invalid. Check down: (3,2) row 3 >= m=3, invalid. So no valid right or down neighbors.
